BoingBoing

# Light Flip is a minimalist phone without the dumb

Image: Light

Light, maker of the stripped-down Light Phones (previously recommended), today announced the Light Flip, a clamshell model that swaps e-ink for matte OLED and gets a hinge, a keypad and (drum roll!) a camera. As much as I loved my Light Phone II, the slow and frustrating e-ink screen ground me down; with OLED, the main tradeoff for responsiveness and clarity will be shorter battery life. [...] Image via CrowdSupply
Oh, Lordy, my wallet: I'm not one to invest in, or generally write about, crowdfunded hardware. I've been burned one too many times. But a decidedly old-school Personal Digital Assistant… READ THE REST

Billionaire Trump aide Elon Musk "ragequit" an online game after repeatedly dying and enduring the mockery of those gamers paying attention. He called them "retards" and logged off Path of… READ THE REST

Elon Musk shows off the Cybertruck's bulletproof windows

## Review: The reMarkable Paper Pro is my favorite tablet [...] The Flip runs LightOS, the company's custom operating system, on a 2.8-inch 480×640 display. The rear camera has a 50-megapixel sensor and 12-megapixel output. Folded, it measures 110mm x 58mm x 19mm and weighs 160 grams. Inside is a MediaTek MT8873 chipset, 6GB of RAM, 128GB of storage, an 1,800 mAh battery, stereo speakers and dual noise-canceling mics. It supports 5G and 4G LTE via nano SIM or eSIM, Bluetooth 5.0, GPS, USB-C and a 3.5mm headphone jack. Software includes T9 predictive text, voice-to-text, audio messaging, directions and a music player, with a developer program now open for community-built tools.

It comes in black, navy, red, pink, yellow and light gray, and will cost $299 outright or "from $39 a month" on Light's service plan. It ships in April 2027.

results.title: LIGHT PHONE FLIP / pros and cons of the new dumb phone
results.content: apps other people have created or even code your own if you know how. Anyways, the one thing I don't really like about the light flip is the fact that they went with an LED screen again instead of e- in, which is super disappointing because that's the whole reason a lot of people fell in love with light in the first place. And even if I'm only using my phone for a few minutes a day, I just don't want to feel like I'm staring at a smartphone or a television, which is why I ultimately switched to the Muda Compact instead of the Light Phone 3. In all fairness though, ENC is not great for taking photos and videos. So, if you're someone who uses a camera phone a lot and wants to see everything in full color, then it probably makes sense for you. Overall, less than half the price for the Light [...] of specs, it's pretty much the same size and weight as a normal flip phone, an OLED display, and a 50 megapixel camera that down samples to 12 megapixels, which seems to be the same one that's on the Light Phone 3, though Light hasn't confirmed that yet. Other than that, the Light Flip also has GPS, Bluetooth, a SIM card slot, and compatibility with major carriers like the previous Liteones. As far as the OS goes, you've got the same zero distraction, zero social media, textbased interface. with the same minimal tools like alarm, timer, calculator, calendar, directory, directions, notes or voice, music, and podcast. That said, since my last light phone review, they've also come out with a developer program where you can load custom apps other people have created or even code your own if [...] the light flip is a great throwback with a faster screen than the light phone 2 and a quality camera. Although again, the LED screen is a little less common and T9 texting is going to be a make orb break feature for a lot of people. Finally, there's the Muda Compact, which is kind of the best of both worlds with a larger, faster Yank screen than the Light Phone 2, as well as a basic camera and side loading support for useful apps like banking, Spotify, and notion, but not the distracting media ones like Instagram. Although the design is not quite as unique as the Light Phone. If you'd like to support the channel, you can shop the furl links down below or watch my Light Phone 2, three, and Moody compact reviews to learn more. results.title: Light Phone Flip review: escape from social media saturation | Wallpaper*
results.content:
Light Phone Flip

Light Phone Flip

(Image credit: The Light Phone)

Hollier calls Light’s Flip a ‘cousin’ to the III. The basic functionality and OS are the same, with a slightly lighter feature set. There’s no NFC sensor or forward-facing selfie camera, for example, nor is the screen touch operated. Instead, there’s a traditional number pad and D-pad controller, with old-school T9 text input. In short, it really prioritises phone calls above all else.

‘Gen Z feel like they were robbed by big tech,’ Hollier explains. ‘Even III feels a bit too much like a smartphone. They tell us that AI is seeping into every aspect of their lives and they hate it.’ (No prizes at all to HMD for recently launching four ‘dumb phones’ with dedicated AI assistant buttons.)

Light Phone Flip [...] Accompanying the launch of the Flip is a biweekly newsletter, Flip Your Life, that taps into the community spirit and helps those who want to make the (perhaps daunting) journey away from a social media world.

Light Phone Flip

Light Phone Flip

(Image credit: The Light Phone)

‘When we originally launched, people didn’t get it,’ Hollier says. ‘Now, they do. But they say, “I wish I could [switch to Light], but….’ So we want to help them.’

‘It’s a process, not a one-time thing,’ Tang acknowledges. ‘Quitting addiction is not an easy process.’

The backlash against an always-online society is slow but steady. Light has made an excellent tool that’s sure to help hurry things along.

Light Phone Flip

Light Phone Flip

(Image credit: The Light Phone) [...] Light Phone Flip

Light Phone Flip

(Image credit: The Light Phone)

Now Light is back, shining its focus on a new sector. Welcome to the Light Phone Flip, the company’s fourth device and its first-ever flip phone. As we’ve often observed, the contemporary flip phone sits within a weird evolutionary niche. On the one hand, there’s the folding screen models typified by Motorola’s razr series or the Samsung Z Flip7 or the Honor Magic V Flip2.

None of these clever pieces of tech does anything to solve the attention issue. To really dig into the disavowal of modern mobile culture, you need to switch to something like HMD’s 2660 Flip 4G, a self-proclaimed dose of digital detox.

Light Phone Flip

Light Phone Flip

(Image credit: The Light Phone)
